title: Security Checklist
intro: Before shipping your task manager, run through this security checklist. Swipe right on practices you should follow, left on dangerous ones.

teach.title: Security Is Not Optional
teach.body: Security isn't a feature you add at the end — it's a mindset throughout development. Common vulnerabilities like SQL injection, XSS (cross-site scripting), and CSRF (cross-site request forgery) are well-understood, and preventing them is straightforward if you follow best practices.

teach.title: Security Is Like Locking Your Doors
teach.body: You don't wait until someone breaks in to install locks. You lock up every night as a habit. Similarly, sanitizing inputs, using HTTPS, and validating on the server should be habits — not afterthoughts you add when something goes wrong.
The top three security habits: validate all input on the server, use parameterized queries (never concatenate SQL), and always use HTTPS in production.

cards.content: Use parameterized queries or an ORM instead of concatenating user input into SQL strings
cards.explanation: Parameterized queries prevent SQL injection by treating user input as data, never as SQL code. This is one of the most important security practices.

cards.content: Store API keys and database credentials directly in your source code for easy access
cards.explanation: Credentials in source code end up in git history and can leak through public repos. Use environment variables and never commit secrets to version control.

cards.content: Set HTTP security headers like Content-Security-Policy and X-Frame-Options on your server
cards.explanation: Security headers protect against XSS, clickjacking, and other browser-based attacks. Most frameworks have middleware that sets these automatically.

cards.content: Disable CORS entirely so any website can call your API — it makes development easier
cards.explanation: Wide-open CORS means any website can make requests to your API on behalf of your users. Configure CORS to only allow your own frontend domain.

cards.content: Rate limit login attempts to prevent brute-force password attacks
cards.explanation: Without rate limiting, attackers can try millions of password combinations. Limiting to 5-10 attempts per minute per IP makes brute force impractical.

cards.content: Show detailed error messages with stack traces to users so they can report bugs more easily
cards.explanation: Stack traces reveal your tech stack, file structure, and potential vulnerabilities. Show generic errors to users and log detailed errors server-side.
